item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The anomaly itself is interesting and unique. Characters having multiple identities and even multiple separate lives is common in fiction, but all these identities/lives being real - simultaneously - is a concept that I don't think I have ever encountered.

In light of this, the fact that 1041 is so utterly barebones becomes all the more frustrating.

This article feels like the beginning of a story that never actually begins, and I don't mean it to say "damn, why is it not explained how 1041 came to be and why is she like that?", because that is not the problem with it. We have a woman that has lived multiple lives across the globe, in wildly different circumstances, all of these circumstances being equally true to outside observers, and nothing comes out of it. Pretty much nothing about her past lives, nothing at all about what her day-to day existence looks like or how the Foundation's outside observers perceive her extraordinary existence, nothing, nothing and a load of nothing with a naught on top.

There is no emotion to be felt from reading this skip, nor any food for thought to take away; all you can take away from 1041 is "wow, sure is a thing that exists" - and while there are many good and enjoyable skips that match such a description, with a premise as fascinating as what you have here, this is not even close to being fulfilling narrative-wise.

-1. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like how this SCP was written. Solid, and it intrigued me from beginning to end. My only question is how they found more than 90 parents who genetically matched SCP-1041, and how they all would have had, supposedly, the same child from all the narratives. Just seemed a bit confusing to me. Overall, I loved of the idea of all the different narratives this women lived through. You'd basically get a new story to listen every time from her. Pretty cool.

+1

P.S. Damn, its been more than 3 years since the last post. I guess either a lot of the users don't post on this SCP or rarely view it. Makes it underrated in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A second reading three years on, and this is all a lot more believable. :) I take it 127 is the 'real' personality, or at least the one that started everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded. I like the SCP, but please keep the object classes as they are. I have no idea what alterius is, and the average frequent reader Joe will not either, not to mention Joe's unwillingness to search what the hell it means.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The author likes to use Latin-based object classes to make the usual safe/euclid/keter ones more specific. (In fact, he's said before that he'd like if more people did that, encouraging creativity and all.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The scip is great, but the "alterius" is outdated. This is a plain Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is pretty good. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I'll buy that. Issue resolved!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool. I never initially planned for either of those to actually factor into the 'mystery', but I like what you've done with 'em.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They looked through medical records of women of the appropriate age losing their left thumb, and 154 turned up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent, techno-babble overload was exactly what I was aiming for.
Now if only I could work in a "reverse the polarity".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The warm glow of your approval has inspired me to complete the set.

-14 and 15 never really got along. Sibling rivalry between twin sisters can be a blood sport But that didn't matter to the man the papers dubbed "The Surgeon". All he cared about was their physical compatibility. The police raid on his cabin only found one survivor. Many papers were published based on forensic examination of the hundreds of grafts and transplants that he inflicted on her, some of which should have been medically impossible to survive. Nearly as many psychological papers have been published regarding her PTSD, survivor's guilt, and DID. The shrinks said it was perfectly normal for her to manifest delusions, believing that she was actually her sister was just the psyche's way of processing the trauma. Their explanations sounded a bit forced when new, fully-formed and impossibly self-consistent personalities emerged; seemingly a new one every day. They had nothing at all to say when the records and valid birth certificates for each of them.

-19 was not adjusting well to her new situation. She wasn't exactly depressed, which is common for new double amputees, she was just no longer engaged in the world. She spent hours alone in her room, just imagining what her life would be like if she had turned left instead of right. Or took the bus instead of driving. Or had gotten her brakes looked at like the manual said. Eventually she was constructing elaborate fantasy lives, each rooted in just a single change to the circumstances of her past. Sometimes, her imaginings were so detailed that she would forget herself entirely, just wandering in her could-have-been selves. Until one day her fantasy was so believable that everyone else, the whole world, reality itself believed it as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad you like them.
But I think you overlooked the last one; I called that the "Omega Option".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but i have one major issue. 154. If the personality isn't displayed by 1041, then how the bugger do we know about it? What made the foundation aware of that one corpse with no listed connection to anything else, and note that it was part of 1041? I just don't get it.

I love the -81 theory wishun presented though. It appeals to my physics brain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This thing makes my brain hurt… An excellent job once again, Scantron.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very cool theories! It would be exceptionally cool if ALL of them were true at the same time, from different universes, leading to the bigger question of "how did such a huge coincidence occur where all of these timestream altering events happened simultaneously in different timestreams?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The -81 theory would have made a lesser man scratch his head in futility. I had a bit of trouble too. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These are super cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Possible explanations
- Based on F-ALL

-Authorities were closing in on 67 so she was attempting to assume a new identity in a very thorough and novel way. She had some records destroyed as prep-work, to lessen the required amount of reality bending. But she prepared too many contingencies so that when the effect was finally triggered all the mystical identity thefts happened together instead of just the one she intended. Should have consulted a better alchemist.
-81 was researching the practical applications of the many-worlds theory of quantum mechanics. But CI experimental protocols are much laxer than at the Foundation. There was an unexpected cross interaction between the multiple anomalies being used in her tests, causing a macro-scale collapse of multiple quantum wave-form potentialities into a single gestalt actuality, with the momentary effective absolute observer as the coalescence point. Things blew up and her parallel selves now share a body; think of a cross between "Quantum Leap" and "Sliders"
-127 is a truly gifted spirit, greater than any other still trapped in the karmic cycle. Her advanced enlightenment and higher plane of spiritual energy was too much for a single reincarnation. But her soul was too pure and the Great Spirits could not bear to shatter it. So in their wisdom, she became the many-in-one.
-154 was a cantankerous, stubborn, spiteful, cruel, leather-souled, poor excuse for a human being. She died and went to the hell she deserved. But Satan couldn't stand her any more than her four ex-husbands could, and heaven surely wasn't going to take her. So she climbed up out of her grave. Fate was insulted and scandalized by this blatant disrespect for his perfect pattern, and so settled on an ironic punishment. 154 wanted another life? Well, Fate would see to it that she got a lot more than she could handle.
-Omega option. All these at once, each in its own parallel world.

I'll have to see if I can come up with something for the other cases. This is fun, thanks!
Oh, and it's also novel and well-written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was unintentional, but I'm glad that the piece has applicability.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know if this was intentional, but it almost feels like this is a commentary on the "nothing is canon" doctrine (perhaps in 1041's case, "everything is canon.") If it is, it's subtle, which is nice. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, the numbers are fucky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only 99? My addition says 182.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for noticing the edit. It's written quite properly, but it just doesn't appeal to me. Which is fine, because it obviously appeals to quite a few others. It's just a case of me having different interests. Have a good day!

Edit: I'm also a victim of extreme Internet courtesy. I've seen how heated things get on here sometimes, and I just want to be respectful to everyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the record, it's not Multiple Personality Disorder, it's Dissociative Identity Disorder. Plus, it's pretty clear that all of these alternate identities are not just being made up by the subject, given that outside people and families distinctly remember that person, not to mention the DNA thing. Plus, you do have to notice that these personae are related to some weird set of narratives, which in turn may or may not both be linked to the missing person in the last line of the skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, feel free to calm down. I've gotten worse responses from people I like less. I won't bite yer head off for stating an opinion.

Not being reeled in by it and thinking that it doesn't feel like an SCP is fine, but I would argue that it's substantially more than a "particularly strange case of Multiple Personality Disorder". For one, let's take the genetic testing of the parents. Those tests are accurate enough that one false positive would be very strange. Either we have a hundred false positives somehow, or these people really did give birth to the exact same person. Like the article says, it's an infinitesimal probability that this is pure chance.

Now, as for the narratives. It's more than a weird case of MPD — this is what you would get if you took a variety of real, living people and smooshed them together into one person. All of those people would have their own paper trails, their own family and acquaintances — none of which are under any obligation to be consistent with each other. Of course, this could be explained as 1041 assimilating all of the appropriate personalities and information somehow, except A) none of those people are exactly around any more, and B) they were all 1041 to begin with, down to the thumb.

E: Just saw your edit. All is well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Null post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this idea, and the article's solidly written, but it feels like it lacks something. It just doesn't quite reel me in. Don't get me wrong, it's professional to the utmost degree. However(and please don't get angry if I'm wrong), this just sounds like a particularly strange case of Multiple Personality Disorder. It doesn't quite feel like an SCP to me. Oh, well. I respect you to the utmost degree, Sir Scantron; this article's just a bit too possible to hook me. I wish you luck.

Edit: Granted, the testimonies from many people does make this more strange, but it's just not that appealing to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some official documents and corroboration from people they knew. Like, a narrative of your life (enough to reasonably prove that you were a real person) could be figured out from your birth certificate, any records of employment you might have, and talking to your family to make sure they had you as a kid. I couldn't think of a better word for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only really anomalous thing I see in this is that the subject has 99 parents;

…I need to make this joke now.

If you're having genealogy problems, I feel bad for ya, son, I got 99 parents, and I'm only one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where do these narratives come from? Are they texts, stories, what? The only really anomalous thing I see in this is that the subject has 99 parents; prior to that detail, it just sounds like someone with an unbelievably consistent and extensive case of MPD.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm intrigued. I do hope you reveal some of the stuff in this later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An earlier draft had the SCP recovered at San Diego International Airport when [DATA EXPUNGED], but I figured that would be a downvote magnet so I removed it. I've elected to leave that question unanswered, since saying exactly how it all came together (pun intended) would reveal more about it than I have planned out, or would want to reveal even if I did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So many questions! So creative! Great stuff! My biggest question that I think the Foundation would try to resolve/explain would be the details of how the petsonalities 'coalesced.' Like how does the Russian one think she was captured? When did her friends and family lose track of her?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Twas a good draft, and tis a good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked it in chat. Still like it here. something something "narrative principles" something something.

Take your +1 you salty dog.
